response: interfaces solve the lack of shared inheritance paths by allowing unrelated classes to implement the same contract without needing a common ancestor like an abstract class would require. architects program to interfaces because they anticipate future file systems or network paths where new objects will emerge that do not fit existing hierarchies. you cannot predict every relationship at design time so extending an abstract class often creates a rigid salad of dependencies that breaks when new requirements appear. programming to an interface decouples the implementation from the usage allowing the system to grow without refactoring the core path logic or dealing with the lack of flexibility found in class inheritance.
